label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's read the passage carefully and see what the question is asking. The question is "What can we learn from the passage?" and the options are A to D. First, the passage is about researchers at the University of Bristol developing intelligent handheld robots. These robots are different from traditional tools because they can know what needs to be done and guide inexperienced users. The key points mentioned are that these robots have more degrees of freedom, allowing them to be more independent and understand the steps being carried out. They cooperate with the user by having the user give directions and the tool performing the detailed task. The passage also states that the aim is to combine the intuitiveness of traditional tools with added intelligence and action. Dr. Mayol-Cuevas mentions three levels of autonomy: no autonomy, semi-autonomous (advises but doesn't act), and fully autonomous (advises and acts, even correcting or refusing incorrect user actions). Now let's look at the options: A) The handheld robots can never disobey the user's order. But the passage says in the fully autonomous level, the robot can refuse incorrect user actions. So this is wrong. B) The autonomy is the only thing that the scientists are considering. The passage mentions autonomy levels but also talks about intuitiveness and physical similarity. So "only" makes this incorrect. C) There will be more intelligence and independence in the handheld robots. The passage says they have more degrees of freedom (independence) and are designed to understand steps, adding intelligence. So this seems correct. D) The aim is to correct and refuse
